title: Cat Pupils Unequal Sizes (Anisocoria): A Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Notice your cat's pupils are different sizes? Learn why anisocoria is a high-priority emergency and how to help your vet with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Anisocoria, or unequal pupil sizes, is a high-priority veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention to prevent permanent vision loss or address potentially life-threatening underlying causes.

What is anisocoria and why are my cat's pupils different sizes?

Anisocoria is the medical term used when a cat's pupils are not the same size. One pupil may be constricted (small), while the other is dilated (large). This condition is a sign that something is interfering with the nerves or structures that control the eye's reaction to light.

Is it an emergency if my cat has unequal pupil sizes?

  • Unequal pupil sizes are considered a high-priority veterinary emergency requiring immediate examination.
  • Potential causes include serious issues such as head trauma, internal eye inflammation, glaucoma, or neurological disease.
  • Delaying treatment can lead to permanent vision loss or indicate a life-threatening brain injury.

Why should I take a photo of my cat's eyes before heading to the clinic?

  • A clear photo is extremely beneficial for triage because pupil sizes can change rapidly depending on light or travel stress.
  • Capturing the exact state of the eyes when you first noticed the problem provides a reliable baseline for the medical team.
  • Having visual evidence helps the veterinarian make a more accurate diagnosis regardless of how the eyes appear upon arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Anisocoria in cats can be associated with dysautonomia, although these cats usually show other clinical signs and are often feline leukemia virus (FeLV)-negative. Pilocarpine (0.1%) applied to the cornea causes profound miosis within 10-15 minutes due to denervation hypersensitivity, but has no effect on a healthy cat. Dilute (0.5%) phenylephrine reverses ptosis and a protruding third eyelid. Midbrain lesions can also cause anisocoria if the sympathetic upper motor neurons and parasympathetic lower motor neurons are both affected, resulting in pupils that are midrange in size and unresponsive to light.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1256)

faqs.question: Could my cat just be born with different sized pupils?
faqs.answer: While rare, some cats have slight variations, but any sudden change in pupil size is always a cause for immediate concern and requires a professional exam.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I watch for?
faqs.answer: Keep an eye out for squinting, redness in the eye, cloudiness, or any changes in your cat's balance and coordination.

faqs.question: Is anisocoria painful for cats?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. While the pupil change itself might not hurt, underlying causes like glaucoma or corneal ulcers can be very painful.
